ocar.opportunity: The explosion of new fluorescent probe technologies—including fluorescent proteins, quantum dots, and organic dyes—created a need for a systematic guide to their properties, tradeoffs, and optimal use cases in cell biology.
ocar.challenge: Choosing the right fluorescent label for a given experiment requires understanding complex tradeoffs between brightness, photostability, phototoxicity, size (which affects protein function), and compatibility with live versus fixed imaging—information scattered across many independent papers.
ocar.action: Giepmans, Adams, Ellisman, and Tsien review all major classes of fluorescent probes available in 2006: genetically encoded fluorescent proteins (GFP and variants), fluorescent protein fusions, quantum dots, chemical dyes (FRET pairs, FlAsH/ReAsH), and SNAP/CLIP tags. They compare brightness, photostability, and suitability for live imaging, FRET, and superresolution.
ocar.resolution: The review provides a systematic comparison framework enabling researchers to match probe classes to experimental requirements. It highlights GFP variants' advantages for live imaging and FRET, quantum dots' superiority for single-particle tracking and multiplexed imaging, and chemical dyes' utility for fixed-tissue applications. The paper catalyzed widespread adoption of genetically encoded probes.
ocar.future_work: Development of probes compatible with combined light-electron microscopy (CLEM) and smaller, brighter genetically encoded tags remained active areas for innovation.

summaries.beginner: Scientists use fluorescent tags to make specific proteins glow so they can be seen under a microscope. This review explains all the different types of glowing labels available and helps scientists choose the right one for their experiment.
summaries.intermediate: Giepmans et al. provide a systematic overview of fluorescent probe classes including GFP and spectral variants, FRET-based sensors, quantum dots, and tetracysteine-biarsenical systems. They compare photophysical properties (brightness, photostability, blinking) and discuss applications including live-cell imaging, FRET-based biosensors, and correlative light-electron microscopy—with specific discussion of how tag size can perturb protein function.
summaries.advanced: This review is foundational for understanding the probe landscape that underlies modern connectomics approaches, particularly CLEM (correlative light and electron microscopy) where fluorescent protein or quantum dot tags are used to identify targets in EM sections. The tradeoffs discussed—particularly between genetically encoded specificity and chemical dye brightness—remain relevant for expansion microscopy and ExFISH applications. The paper predates mEos, Dendra, and other photoconvertible proteins widely used in super-resolution and CLEM workflows.

discussion_prompts: How has the development of photoconvertible and photoswitchable fluorescent proteins (e.g., Dendra2, mEos) expanded experimental capabilities beyond what was described in this 2006 review?
What are the key considerations when choosing a fluorescent label for correlative light-electron microscopy (CLEM) workflows in connectomics?
How do quantum dot properties (blinking, size, surface chemistry) compare to fluorescent proteins for single-synapse tracking in live tissue?
